frameworks.name: model-based reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: on-the-fly planning over a learned state-transition model supporting flexible adjustment

frameworks.name: model-free reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: cached state-action policy contrasted as the less flexible alternative

frameworks.name: neural replay
frameworks.one_line: sequential reactivation of state representations measured by MEG as a planning substrate

core_question: Do on-task (online) and off-task (offline) neural replay support the same form of planning or do they support distinct, dissociable decision strategies?
key_assumptions: MEG-decoded sequential state representations index replay
Model-based vs model-free planning can be dissociated by responses to reward and structural task changes
Replay events can be detected via lagged cross-correlations between decoded state probabilities
manipulations: Changed reward values associated with states mid-experiment
Instructed swap of state positions to invalidate prior policy
Interleaved 1-move vs 2-move trials requiring different optimal first moves
Compared on-task replay following outcomes vs off-task replay during rest
key_findings: More flexible subjects (higher index of flexibility) showed more on-task replay coupled with re-planning of encoded trajectories
Less flexible subjects showed more off-task (rest) replay of previously preferred trajectories
On-task replay was associated with subsequent policy change at the replayed state
Off-task replay favored trajectories that had generated larger reward prediction errors
interpretations: Online and offline replay both participate in planning but support dissociable strategies — online replay subserves flexible model-based re-planning while offline replay consolidates a less flexible cached policy
limitations_noted: Causal role of the two replay modes in behavior remains to be established
